questions.question: Explain the fundamental concept behind Copy-on-Write (COW) and describe one primary motivation for its use in operating systems.
keyPoints: Copy-on-Write is an optimization strategy where a process shares a single copy of data with its parent or other processes until one process modifies it.

When a process attempts to write to the shared resource, the OS intervenes, creates a separate private copy of that data for the modifying process, and then allows the write to proceed on the new copy.
A primary motivation is to save memory and reduce overhead by deferring the copying of data until it is absolutely necessary, improving performance in scenarios like process forking.

questions.question: Describe the initial steps the kernel takes when a `fork()` system call is made on a system that uses Copy-on-Write. What happens to the page table entries?
keyPoints: The kernel creates a new process (child) that is a duplicate of the calling process (parent).
Instead of immediately copying all the parent's pages, the kernel marks the writable pages of both processes as read-only and sets them to be shared via Copy-on-Write.

The page table entries for both the parent and child processes are updated to point to the same physical memory frames, but with their permissions changed to read-only to trigger a page fault on a write attempt.

questions.question: Analyze the sequence of events that occurs from the moment a process in a COW-forked pair attempts to write to a shared memory page until the write operation is completed. Include the role of the MMU and the page fault handler.
keyPoints: The process issues a write instruction to a memory address that is mapped to a read-only COW page.
The Memory Management Unit (MMU) detects an invalid write operation to a read-only page and triggers a page fault exception, transferring control to the operating system's page fault handler.

The page fault handler identifies the fault as a COW event. It then allocates a new physical page frame, copies the content of the original shared page to this new frame, and updates the process's page table to map the virtual address to the new physical frame with write permissions.
Finally, the handler returns control to the process, which re-executes the write instruction, which now succeeds on its private copy.
